title: Your English Journey Map: What You'll Learn This Year!
simple_explanation: This page is like the list of all the cool adventures you'll go on in English class this year! It shows you the names of all the stories you'll read, the writing you'll do, and the poems you'll learn, all sorted into big sections called Terms.
detailed_explanation: The book is divided into three main parts called 'Terms,' just like your school year has different terms. Each Term has several 'Units,' and each Unit focuses on a specific type of English, like reading 'fiction' stories, writing 'non-fiction' articles, or exploring 'poetry'. For example, in Term 1, you'll dive into stories that use 'flashbacks' (where characters remember past events) and learn about writing 'biographies' (stories about other people's lives) and 'autobiographies' (stories about your own life). You'll also explore 'figurative language' in poetry. At the end of each term, there's a 'Review' section. This is a chance for you to check what you've learned and see how much you've grown in your English skills!

examples_expanded.text: In **Term 1**, you might read a story where a character suddenly remembers something important from their past (a flashback). You'll also learn how to write about someone famous, like a sports hero, or even write a short story about your own life.
examples_expanded.context: This example shows how we use the concept of overview of english learning topics in a real sentence.

examples_expanded.text: In **Term 2**, you could explore cool 'science fiction' stories about space travel or robots, and learn how to write a good 'argument' to convince someone of your point of view.
examples_expanded.context: This example shows how we use the concept of overview of english learning topics in a real sentence.

examples_expanded.text: In **Term 3**, you'll get to read 'playscripts' and learn how to write 'persuasive articles' for a newspaper or magazine.
examples_expanded.context: This example shows how we use the concept of overview of english learning topics in a real sentence.

guided_practice: Identify the meaning of 'Contents' in a sentence. Remember it means: A list of the chapters or topics in a book, usually found at the beginning.
Match the word 'Term' with a similar concept from your own experience.
Look at the first example again. How would you modify it to make it your own?
application_tasks: Create 3 of your own sentences using the core idea of Overview of English Learning Topics.
Imagine you are teaching this topic to a friend. Write down exactly what you would say.
challenge_tasks: Combine the concept of 'Contents' with something you learned in a previous chapter to write a short paragraph.
Can you find an exception to this rule or a creative way to bend it? Explain how.
key_points: The Contents page is your complete guide to the entire book.
The book is organised into three terms, each with specific units of study.
Units cover different areas of English: fiction, non-fiction, and poetry.
Review sections are included to help you assess your understanding and progress.

vocabulary.definition: A list of the chapters or topics in a book, usually found at the beginning.
A period of time in the school year during which classes are held.
A section of the book focusing on a particular topic or skill.
Stories that are invented and not based on real events or facts.
Writing that is based on facts, real events, and real people.
A type of writing that uses language in a special way to express feelings or ideas, often with rhythm and rhyme.
Scenes in a story that show something that happened earlier, interrupting the present action.
A story written about someone else's life.
A story written about your own life.
Language that uses words or expressions with a meaning that is different from the literal interpretation (e.g., similes, metaphors).
Language that creates vivid pictures or sensory experiences in the reader's mind.
The written text of a play, including dialogue and stage directions.
Articles written with the aim of convincing the reader to agree with a particular point of view or take a specific action.
